flashcards.front: What is a bus in computer architecture?
flashcards.back: A bus is a set of conducting wires that transmits data, address, and control signals between different components of the computer system such as CPU, memory, and I/O devices.

flashcards.front: What is the function of the DATA BUS?
flashcards.back: The data bus carries actual data between the CPU, memory, and I/O devices. It is bidirectional and its width determines how many bits can be transferred simultaneously.

flashcards.front: What is the function of the ADDRESS BUS?
flashcards.back: The address bus carries memory addresses from the CPU to memory and I/O devices. It is unidirectional and determines the maximum memory capacity that can be addressed (2^n addresses for n address lines).

flashcards.front: What is the function of the CONTROL BUS?
flashcards.back: The control bus carries control signals that manage the communication between components, including signals like READ, WRITE, MEMORY READ, I/O READ, INTERRUPT, and CLOCK signals.
The system bus is the main bus that connects the CPU to main memory. It consists of the data bus, address bus, and control bus, forming the backbone of internal communication.
Bus arbitration is the process of resolving conflicts when multiple devices want to use the bus simultaneously. It ensures that only one device controls the bus at any given time.
Bus width refers to the number of data lines (bits) that can be transmitted simultaneously. A 32-bit bus can transfer 32 bits of data in parallel, affecting system performance.
A bus cycle is the basic time unit for bus operations, representing one complete read or write operation. It typically includes address, data, and wait states.
A synchronous bus uses a clock signal to coordinate all operations. All devices on the bus operate in synchronization with this clock, making timing predictable and simpler to implement.
An asynchronous bus does not use a common clock. It uses handshaking signals between devices to coordinate operations, allowing connection of devices with different speeds.

flashcards.front: What is BUS ARBITRATION using DAISY CHAIN method?
flashcards.back: In daisy chain arbitration, devices are connected in priority order. Each device has a grant line that passes to the next device if it does not request the bus. The highest priority requesting device gets the bus.
In polling arbitration, the CPU or arbitration unit checks each device sequentially to see if it is requesting the bus. Devices are checked in a fixed priority order.
In independent request arbitration, each device has separate request and grant lines connected to an arbiter. The arbiter evaluates all requests and grants the bus based on priority logic.
Bus mastering is the ability of a device to take control of the bus and initiate bus cycles independently, without CPU intervention. DMA controllers and intelligent I/O devices use bus mastering.
Bus protocol defines the rules and timing for communication on the bus, including signal levels, timing sequences, and command encoding that devices must follow to exchange data.
